dbBikes.motor: 500W (900W peak) geared rear hub, torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: Torque-sensor commuter workhorse. Ships loaded with rack, fenders, Zoom Aria fork, and Tektro hydros, so takeoffs skew toward comfort parts.
Comfort cruiser with torque sensor and turn signals. Rigid fork and relaxed geometry; takeoffs concentrate in contact points and the BC280 display.
46lb budget city bike with a torque sensor. Mechanical discs are the number-one owner complaint, making brake takeoffs the signature Soltera trade-in.

dbBikes.motor: Bosch Active Line 40Nm mid-drive (2019-2023, Gen 1-2); Bosch Active Line Plus 50Nm Smart System (2024+, Gen 3)
dbBikes.battery: Bosch PowerPack 400 external (2019-2023); Bosch Compact PowerTube 400 internal (2024+)
dbBikes.blurb: Trek's entry Bosch commuter and one of the most common premium e-hybrids on the road. Huge parc means steady takeoff flow: Purion displays, 4A chargers, and stock Bontrager contact points come off constantly.

dbBikes.motor: 8kW peak mid-mounted IPM (2023-2025); Evo/Expert refresh adds transfer-case gearbox, Expert claims +10% peak power (2026)
dbBikes.blurb: Sting MX4 and Sting R MX4 are the same bike per GritShift and Talaria dealers, so one entry covers both names. Sur-Ron's main rival with the same strip-it-day-one owner culture. The 2026 Evo/Expert refresh adds a gearbox and, on Expert, a genuinely valuable Manitou fork.
Sur-Ron derived platform, and many parts physically interchange with the Light Bee X. Same upgrade culture, same flooded takeoff market.
The flagship Super73 with full adjustable suspension. Owners customize hard, but the parts culture is cosmetic and comfort driven rather than performance teardown.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm); Stealth M24 with torque sensor on 2.0 (2025+)
dbBikes.battery: 48V 14Ah (672Wh) single or dual on 1.0 (2023-2024); 48V 13Ah (624Wh) single or dual, 48V 17.5Ah long-range dual option on 2.0 (2025+)
dbBikes.blurb: The budget cargo workhorse, 450 lb payload on the 2.0. Families load it up and upgrade range and rubber first. The rack is part of the frame, so takeoffs skew batteries, tires, and contact points.

dbBikes.motor: 750W Bafang geared rear hub (80Nm), torque + cadence sensors
dbBikes.battery: 52V 19.2Ah (998Wh) pack; case style changed G1 to G2 mid-run
dbBikes.blurb: Fast 52V commuter from a brand that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024. Owners cannot buy OEM anything anymore, so working takeoffs, above all batteries, displays, and controllers, carry real scarcity value. Price and hold accordingly.

dbBikes.motor: Specialized 2.0 (Brose-based) 70Nm mid-drive, both eras (2022-2025 gen and the 2026 redesign both list 250W/70Nm)
dbBikes.battery: Specialized U2-710 710Wh (2022-2025); 710Wh frame-integrated pack in the redesigned 2026 frame (cross-generation interchange NOT verified)
dbBikes.blurb: Class 3 flagship commuter with the quiet Brose-based 2.0 motor and a big 710Wh pack. Proprietary electronics mean Specialized takeoffs command replacement-part money from stranded owners.
Rad's flagship fat bike, sold in huge numbers from 2021 on. Semi-integrated battery, dual displays, hydraulic brakes. Takeoff supply is deep and parts demand stays steady.
Commuter workhorse with the RadRover 6 Plus electronics in a 27.5 package. Owners swap contact points fast and the battery holds value.
The budget moped-style runabout. Single speed, LED control pad, external battery. Cheap bike, so takeoffs are cheap, except the battery.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub, 60Nm (Shengyi DGW22 2020-2022 -> Bafang ~2022-mid 2023 -> AKM angled-gear from Aug 2023)
dbBikes.blurb: Ride1Up's long-running flagship commuter, discontinued early 2025 in favor of the Vorsa; huge installed base means steady takeoff flow of Selle Royal saddles, Schwalbe tires and Tektro brakes.

dbBikes.motor: 750W (1130W peak) geared rear hub, 80Nm, torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: Aventon's best-selling fat-tire all-rounder. Huge install base means steady takeoff supply: knobby tires, wedge saddles, and Zoom forks come off constantly.
81lb longtail cargo hauler with a 143lb-rated rack and hydraulic dropper post. Takeoffs skew utilitarian; the 720Wh pack is the money part.
Sub-$3k mid-drive eMTB with name-brand parts (RockShox, SRAM, Maxxis). The only Aventon whose takeoffs have a genuine enthusiast resale market.
Comfort-first step-thru with a UL-listed 692Wh pack. Mechanical brakes and a flimsy fork (reviewers' words) define the upgrade path and the takeoff stream.
UL-listed fat-tire value bike. CST BFT knobbies come off for street tires constantly, and the shared display and battery SKUs help takeoff liquidity.
36lb stealth urban bike with a frame-integrated non-removable battery. Battery is NOT a takeoff candidate; the trade-in stream is pure contact points and tires.

dbBikes.motor: 5.4kW rear hub motor (72V era); 80V hub motor, 18kW max, on 2024+ RCR 80V
dbBikes.blurb: Cafe-racer moped with real motorcycle speed. Loyal owner base mods steadily but the parts market is a fraction of the Sur-Ron world, so resale is slow and thin.

dbBikes.motor: Bosch Performance Line CX 75Nm (MY2020, re-rated 85Nm by firmware for 2021-2022); Bosch Smart System Performance Line CX 85Nm (2023+, Gen 2)
dbBikes.battery: Bosch PowerTube 500 (2020-2022); Bosch Smart System PowerTube 500 or 625 depending on config (2023+)
dbBikes.blurb: Trek's serious commuter with the integrated PowerTube and full rack, fender, and light kit. Owners upgrade contact points fast and part-outs feed a real PowerTube and Purion takeoff market.
The X260's smaller sibling, big with the teen rider crowd. Parts demand exists but skews cheap, and the buyer pool is smaller than the 60V bikes.
The volume seller of the Super73 lineup. Big install base means steady takeoff flow, but parts are cheap new so used prices stay modest.
Entry-level Super73 with no suspension and a smaller pack. Least teardown culture in the lineup, so takeoff volume and values both run light.
Full-suspension R-series in the Brooklyn trim. Shares the S2/R parts bin, so most takeoffs merge into existing pools.
Full-size 90V flagship, still in production but thin on the ground in the US. Takeoff parts exist in small numbers and resale evidence is nearly nonexistent.

dbBikes.motor: 300W geared rear hub, Stealth M24 (819W peak, 28Nm)
dbBikes.blurb: Lectric's entry folder, refreshed in 2024 with hydraulic brakes and a color display. Single speed keeps it simple. Same comfort-swap pattern as the XP: smaller parts, smaller money.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm); Stealth M24 with torque sensor on 2.0 (2025+)
dbBikes.blurb: Lectric's fat tire adventure bike with an RST Renegade fork and hydraulic brakes stock. Riders who use it as intended chew through tires and want more range. Lock-on grips and real suspension mean fewer comfort swaps than the XP line.

dbBikes.motor: 500W mid-mounted jackshaft motor driving a rear differential (1092W peak)
dbBikes.blurb: The default budget electric trike until the Trike2 replaced it in 2025. Buyers skew older and want backrest saddles almost on day one. Hydraulic brakes, single speed, and the XP-pattern battery keep parts interchangeable and moving.

dbBikes.motor: 750W Bafang geared rear hub (80Nm; ~1,036W peak on 2018 spec, ~1,300W peak quoted on later 52V bikes)
dbBikes.battery: 48V 13Ah (624Wh) standard on early bikes (2018-2019, 52V optional); 52V 19.2Ah (998Wh) standard on later bikes (~2020-2024)
dbBikes.blurb: Juiced's best seller: a 28 mph fat tire commuter with a Shimano 9-speed and hydraulic brakes. The brand's 2024 collapse turned every working takeoff into supported-life insurance for stranded owners. Batteries and controllers are the money parts.

Full-suspension 52V moped, orphaned when Juiced folded in 2024. Almost nothing on it cross-shops with standard bike parts, which cuts both ways: takeoffs are scarce and wanted, but the only buyers are other Scorpion owners.
Cannondale's step-thru Bosch commuter, the Verve+ 2's direct rival. Same Purion and 2A charger story: predictable takeoffs and a guaranteed charger upsell.
Upright comfort cruiser on the same MasterMind platform as the Vado. Comfort buyers swap saddles and grips immediately, and the 530 to 710 battery upgrade is a known path.

dbBikes.motor: Giant SyncDrive Sport (Yamaha PWseries ST) 70-80Nm (2020-2022); Giant SyncDrive Sport2 75Nm (2023+ redesign)
dbBikes.blurb: Giant's Yamaha-powered trekking commuter. Proprietary Giant electronics mean thinner takeoff demand than Bosch parts, but batteries and chargers still find captive replacement buyers.

dbBikes.motor: 6kW peak mid-mounted IPM with belt primary
dbBikes.blurb: Talaria's 17in supermoto pit bike, sold as X3 or xXx. Newer and lower volume than the Sting, so the used parts market is thin and prices must stay careful.

dbBikes.motor: 750W (1100W peak) geared rear hub, 75Nm, SensorSwap torque/cadence
dbBikes.blurb: Feature-dense commuter: torque sensor, 3.5in color display, hydraulic brakes, full accessory kit. Well-specced stock, so takeoffs concentrate in contact points.

The best selling budget folder in America from 2022 to 2024, replaced by the XP4 in 2025. Owners swap the same four parts in month one: saddle, seatpost, pedals, tires. Takeoff supply is deep and buyer demand from budget repairers is steady.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak, 85Nm, Stealth M24)
dbBikes.blurb: Torque-sensor commuter at a throwaway price, with an SR Suntour fork and Shimano Altus 8-speed stock. Contact-point swaps dominate; the fork and mech mostly come off when a bike gets parted out. Replaced by the XPress2 in May 2026.
Gen 1 RadRunner with the works: LCD, suspension fork, fenders, passenger-ready. Discontinued, so its parts feed the big RadRunner fleet still on the road.
The 22-inch-wheel cargo hauler with the infamous tire recall. Check the sidewall before crediting any tire. Everything else trades like standard Rad kit.
2024 cargo redo: torque sensor, 720Wh Safe Shield pack, hydraulic brakes, standard 20-inch wheels. Too new for a real takeoff market yet.
Discontinued single-speed city bike on a lighter 500W platform. Batteries are the only takeoff with real legs; owners hunt spares.
The classic external-battery fat bike that built Rad. Biggest mod community of the bunch; Bolton kits, tire swaps and hydraulic conversions are routine.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (Shengyi at Feb-2023 launch per Ebike Escape pre-release review; AKM listed on later spec sheets per EBR/Ride1Up)
dbBikes.battery: 48V 12.8Ah LG-cell pack (2023); 48V 14Ah (2024 onward, shared with LMT'D V2)
dbBikes.blurb: The entry commuter that reviewers keep calling the value pick - launched Feb 2023 at $1,295, now $1,095; owners upgrade the budget shifting and coil fork first, and the no-name takeoffs are worth little.
The cheapest real Brose mid-drive in the US market; parts spec is a tier above the rest of this list, so its takeoffs (Tektro 4-piston hydros, microShift Advent, Maxxis) actually hold some value.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub with torque sensor (V1 2021-2023; V2 from Mar 2024 with quieter angled-gear motor)
dbBikes.battery: 48V 14Ah / 672Wh both generations (V1: Reention Eel, Samsung cells; V2: shares the Turris 14Ah pack)
dbBikes.blurb: The torque-sensor commuter that rides a tier above its price; V1 shipped an 80mm air fork, V2 a Suntour XCM32 coil, and the Samsung-cell battery is the best takeoff on the bike either way.
Best-selling budget folding fat bike; everything on it is no-name except the Shimano 7-speed, so takeoffs are near worthless but the saddle, tires and seatpost churn constantly.
28 mph step-thru folder sold direct and through Amazon; rare 750W folding format, but the component takeoffs are the same near-worthless budget fare as the Mars.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1000W peak, cadence sensor 2021-2023; Engine Pro 2.0 from 2024: 1200W peak, torque sensor)
dbBikes.blurb: 83 lb full-suspension budget folder that reviewers call a great canvas for customization; the big 16Ah battery and the controller are the only takeoffs with a pulse - and both split 48V/52V across the 2.0 boundary.

dbBikes.motor: Bosch Active Line Plus 50Nm (2019-2023); Bosch Smart System Active Line Plus 50Nm (2024+)
dbBikes.battery: Bosch PowerPack 400 rack-mounted (2019-2020); Bosch PowerTube 500 in-frame (approx 2021-2023); Bosch PowerPack 545 on-tube Smart System (2024+)
dbBikes.blurb: Gazelle's entry Bosch commuter with a standard Shimano 9-speed drivetrain. Ships with the slow 2A compact charger, which makes the charger upgrade the most predictable sale on this bike.

dbBikes.motor: Mid-mounted PMSM: 12.5kW peak (2023-2024); V2/HP hairpin-wound motor 18kW sustained, 21kW peak (2025-2026)
dbBikes.blurb: The grown-up Sur-Ron. Better stock parts than the Light Bee but owners still chase Sirris suspension and EBMX power, so takeoffs flow steadily.
The $1,050 bestseller of budget folding fat bikes; entry-level everything, and the mechanical disc brakes are the one takeoff nearly every owner replaces.
2023 refresh of the RadRunner with hydraulic brakes, an RST suspension fork, 7-speed drivetrain and a semi-integrated battery. Newer electronics mean a thin used market for now.
33 lb stealth belt-drive single-speed, replaced by the V3 in late 2024; minimalist build means few takeoffs, and the battery is sealed in the frame so it never trades.
Folding 20x4 runabout on the classic Rad external-battery platform. LED control pad, mechanical discs, rigid fork. Same electronics as the old fleet, so parts cross over.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1310W peak), '99% quieter' motor, XPerience torque/cadence switch
dbBikes.blurb: The May 2026 rework of Lectric's torque-sensor commuter. Same recipe as the XPress 750 - Suntour fork, Altus 8-speed, hydraulics - with a quieter motor and a sensor-mode switch. Zero used-parts history yet; everything priced at the floor.

dbBikes.motor: 750W geared rear hub (1188W peak, 1440W peak in Boost; 80Nm), torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: Aventon's fat-tire flagship reborn on the 36V ACU smart platform. Keyless battery, torque sensor with boost, and a suspension seatpost from the factory. The shared 733Wh pack has the widest fitment Aventon has ever shipped, which will make it the money takeoff.

dbBikes.motor: 500W geared rear hub (864W peak, 60Nm), bottom-bracket torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: The Pace line's 2025 ACU-era reboot: torque sensor, keyless shared battery, hydraulics, and a suspension seatpost, step-thru only. Cruiser buyers swap contact points and little else, so the shared 733Wh pack and BC147 display carry the value.

dbBikes.battery: 48V 15Ah (720Wh) Safe Shield Advanced semi-integrated, Samsung 50GB/LG M50LT cells, UL 2271
dbBikes.blurb: Rad's torque-sensor Class 3 commuter on the 2024 Safe Shield platform - the most modern Rad in the fleet. The UL-certified semi-integrated 720Wh pack and the new color display are the money parts, and the post-bankruptcy parts squeeze only helps them.

dbBikes.battery: 48V 15Ah (720Wh) Safe Shield Advanced semi-integrated, Samsung 50GB/LG M50LT cells, UL 2271
dbBikes.blurb: The off-road half of the Radster pair: mid-fat 27.5x3 rubber and trail geometry on the same Safe Shield platform. Battery, display, and brakes pool with the Road, which is exactly what you want for inventory turnover.

The 2025 rebirth of Rad's moped-style runabout with a UL Safe Shield pack, hydraulics, and the passenger kit in the box. Gen-1 RadRunner parts do NOT cross over. Too new for a used market; the external Safe Shield pack is the part to watch.

The radar bike. Rad's flagship utility runabout with a UL semi-integrated pack and a display that watches traffic behind you. The radar-display stack is genuinely new hardware with no aftermarket substitute - the single most interesting takeoff on any 2025 Rad.

dbBikes.motor: 750W (1100W peak) geared rear hub, 75Nm, SensorSwap torque/cadence
dbBikes.blurb: Velotric's best-seller rebuilt for 2026 with a real air fork at $1,999 - unheard of in this class. The bigger 730Wh pack breaks compatibility with the old three-model battery pool, so grade it as its own part from day one.

Velotric goes full suspension: 120mm air fork, DNM shock, and the biggest pack the brand has shipped. A budget full-sus fat bike will get ridden hard, which means crash part-outs - the fork, shock, and 802Wh pack are the parts with a future.

dbBikes.motor: AKM angled-gear 750W rear hub (1380W peak, 95Nm), Intui-Drive torque + cadence sensors (switchable)
dbBikes.blurb: Ride1Up's do-everything replacement for the 700 Series: torque-and-cadence switchable, touchscreen display, and a UL Samsung pack under $1,600. Name-brand rubber and the touchscreen give its takeoffs more life than the usual budget fare.

dbBikes.motor: 500W angled-gear rear hub, 50Nm, Intui-Drive torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: The stealth Roadster grows up: torque sensor, removable Samsung pack, and a Dayco belt instead of Gates. The removable battery finally gives this line a battery takeoff, and the Advent trim adds a real derailleur to the pool.

dbBikes.motor: 8kW peak mid-mounted motor, 185Nm max wheel torque, vector sine-wave controller
dbBikes.blurb: Sur-Ron's new-for-2026 minibike. Nearly-new takeoffs will trickle out as the mod crowd gets hold of it, but there is no resale history yet - buy at floors only.
Talaria's 72V flagship, pitched at the Ultra Bee. Young parts market with almost no resale history - price at floors and let the first real solds teach us.
Talaria's new full-size 96V enduro, launched December 2025. Real dirt bike dimensions mean its tires and controls have crossover demand, but the parts market itself does not exist yet.

dbBikes.motor: 500W (1092W peak, 55Nm) or 750W (1310W peak, 85Nm) geared rear hub, in-house torque sensor
dbBikes.blurb: The 2025 successor to the XP 3.0 and the presumptive volume king. First XP with a standard suspension fork, torque sensor, hydraulics, and a color display. Too new for a used-parts market, so everything is priced at the floor until comps exist.

dbBikes.motor: Mid-mounted PMSM: 5kW peak (2018-2021), 6kW peak (2022-2025); 10kW peak 72V PMSM, 295Nm (2026+)
dbBikes.blurb: The default halo e-moto. Every owner strips it in month one, so takeoff supply is bottomless and resale is slow. The 2026 72V overhaul resets the parts platform - treat 2026 takeoffs as a new family.

dbBikes.battery: 5.2kWh lithium pack (nominal voltage not yet verified)
dbBikes.blurb: Talaria's flagship, a 28kW halo bike sold alongside the Komodo. Too new for a parts market - catalog carefully, price at floors, and verify the battery label because published specs disagree.

dbBikes.motor: Electric dirt bike, three selectable power modes (150/200/300cc-equivalent); full specs unpublished pre-launch
dbBikes.blurb: Segway's CES 2026 return to dirt bikes, replacing the X260 era. Ships mid-2026 - this entry exists so the first takeoffs have somewhere to land, not because a parts market exists.
The smallest of Super73's new-for-2026 A-Series. Commuter culture, not teardown culture - expect light takeoff flow led by tire and battery spares.
The mid-size of the 2026 A-Series and the likely volume seller. Same commuter parts story as its siblings - thin takeoff flow until the install base grows.
The tall-rider platform of the 2026 A-Series. Largest wheels in the lineup; otherwise the same commuter parts economics as its siblings.

dbBikes.motor: Bosch Performance Line 65Nm (2020-2022; C380+ trim ran Performance Line Speed 85Nm); Bosch Smart System Performance Line 75Nm (2023+)
dbBikes.battery: Bosch PowerTube 500 (2020-2022); Bosch Smart System PowerTube 625 (2023+)
dbBikes.blurb: Dutch belt-drive commuter with the Enviolo stepless hub and Bosch Performance Line. Near zero drivetrain takeoffs since the belt lasts forever, but the Bosch electronics and quality contact points move.

dbCatalog.name: Rad Safe Shield 48V 13Ah external battery (624Wh)
dbCatalog.comp_note: NEW row: 13Ah Safe Shield Advanced external is a new size for the 2025 RadRunner generation (Electrek 2025-04-30) - not the 14Ah legacy external, not the RadWagon 5's 15Ah. UL-listed, Range Extender ecosystem. Post-bankruptcy parts uncertainty supports demand; no used solds (Jul 2026). Estimated $200.

dbCatalog.name: Stock 48V 10-14Ah battery pack
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The range-anxiety cure: bigger 52V pack

dbCatalog.name: Stock 500-750W hub motor (wheel included)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Hub-to-mid-drive is the big-league swap

dbCatalog.name: Stock controller
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: More amps, smoother throttle mapping

dbCatalog.name: Stock tire pair (street or fat)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Street rubber transforms fat-tire commuters

dbCatalog.name: Stock mechanical disc brake set
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The #1 upgrade on every cable-brake bike

dbCatalog.name: Stock hydraulic brake set (MT200 class)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: MT200s work; MT5e's stop a loaded bike

dbCatalog.name: Stock LCD display
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: App-programmable and half the size

dbCatalog.name: Shimano CUES 9-speed rear derailleur (takeoff)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Shimano CUES 9-speed verified on 2024-2026 dealer listings. CUES is Shimano's current mid-line and Linkglide parts have real replacement demand, but used takeoffs are still a $20-30 commodity; no solds pulled. New row — also the future home for CUES takeoffs off Verve+ 2 Gen 3 and Allant+ 7 Gen 2 intakes.

dbCatalog.name: Bontrager 27.5x2.4 tire (takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Verified $63-74 street. The flat-proof commuter default.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Dealer spec: 27.5in wheels with 2.4in puncture-resistant tires; exact Bontrager model name not verified, so the row is named by verified size only. House-brand takeoff tires move slow and cheap; floor per tire.

dbCatalog.name: Specialized MasterMind H3 display (takeoff)
dbCatalog.comp_note: NEW 2026 row: MasterMind H3 verified on Specialized's 2026 Vado listings (Apple Find My integration, motor locking, OTA updates). New-generation part with zero used-market history; estimated above the TCD's $120 on newer-part scarcity. Does NOT fit 2022-2025 Vados.

dbCatalog.name: Zoom 80mm hydraulic suspension fork (Aventure)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The stock Zoom is a pogo stick with a lockout. The USD-8 actually damps instead of just bouncing.
dbCatalog.comp_note: eBay Zoom fork listings run $24-95 used but none are confirmed Aventure-spec solds. Priced low; unbranded OEM forks move slowly. Same fork across the 2023-2025 .2 run; no revision evidenced, no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 720Wh battery (Aventure.2)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Proprietary mount, so a second OEM pack is the only real play. Double your range, keep the warranty.
dbCatalog.comp_note: New from Aventon ~$550; generic 48V 15Ah packs sell new on eBay for $170-265, which caps what buyers pay for a used proprietary pack. One eBay sold confirmed but price hidden. Priced low. Same 720Wh pack carried over from gen 1 through the whole .2 run (Ebike Escape); no capacity or connector change evidenced, no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon BC280 color display
dbCatalog.comp_note: New retail $69.99 direct from Aventon. Two eBay used listings found (Pace 500 and Sinch 2 fitment) but no confirmed sold prices. Buyers are owners with cracked screens. BC 280 was fitted from the Jan 2023 launch; no display revision evidenced within the .2 run (revisions were generational: gen 1 monochrome LCD, Aventure 3 ACU-era display), so no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon Pace cruiser saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Riders past 15 miles a day still want the big spring.
dbCatalog.comp_note: CARRY-OVER: the Pace line keeps its thicker rounded cruiser seat; merges with the existing Pace row. Slightly better than wedge resale, still a saddle.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 26x4 stock knobby tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Knobbies hum on pavement. Speedsters cut the noise and add range.
dbCatalog.comp_note: CARRY-OVER: same 26x4 knobby size and brand family as the Aventure.2, merges with the existing row. Street-tire conversions keep takeoff supply high.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock ergonomic lock-on grips
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Numb hands come from round grips. Forty bucks fixes it.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Parts-bin value; merges with the fleet-wide row.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock alloy platform pedals
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Stock pedal bearings die young on the fat bikes.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Shared OEM platforms; merges with the fleet-wide row.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon rear rack (Aventure)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Rack became included-in-base-price at the .2 launch (Ebike Escape: gen-1 sold it separately), so every 2023-2025 bike has one. Demand is thin since every bike ships with it. Priced accordingly.

dbCatalog.name: Zoom Aria suspension fork (Level.2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Travel corrected to 65mm coil per EBR's Level.2 review. Zoom forks list $24-95 used on eBay with no confirmed Aria solds. Commuters rarely swap it, so supply and demand are both thin. Same fork 2022-2026; no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 672Wh battery (Level)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Commuters kill packs with daily charging. A second OEM battery doubles range and halves cycle wear.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Facebook Marketplace listing referenced $450 new retail for the Level pack. Generic 48V packs at $170-265 new cap the used ceiling. Priced low. 672Wh spec constant across the run (Level 3's 733Wh pack is a different 36V system on a separate model); no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 27.5x2.1 stock tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: A commuter with a flat is a paperweight. Marathon Plus is the boring correct answer.
dbCatalog.comp_note: 27.5x2.1 confirmed by EBR. No resale datapoints found for the OEM tire specifically. Unbranded takeoff commuter pairs move at $20-30. Priced low.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon rigid alloy seatpost
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: No rear suspension means your spine is the shock. The NCX does the job for under a hundred bucks.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Suspension seatpost is THE Level.2 comfort mod, so rigid takeoff posts pile up. Almost no buyers; priced at floor.

dbCatalog.name: Tektro hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Level.2)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Model pinned down in v2: Ebike Escape and EBR both identify Tektro HD-E350 hydros with 180mm rotors, constant across the run. New HD-E350 sets run $85-160, so used takeoff sets land near $45. Takeoffs mostly come from crash repairs. No year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 614Wh battery (Pace 500)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Only OEM packs fit the frame slot. Second battery beats range anxiety.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Smaller pack than Aventure/Level so priced below them. No confirmed solds; generic new packs cap the ceiling. Priced low. 614Wh spec constant 2023-2025 (Pace 4 moved to 36V on a separate model); no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Bafang 750W rear hub motor (27.5, Juiced)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Bafang hub motor wheels list $120-250 on eBay new and used; laced takeoffs priced at the low end pending sold data.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon stock wedge saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Still the default fix on r/aventon.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Shared Aventon wedge family; merges with the fleet-wide row. Minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Aventure/Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: 78 lb at 28 mph still deserves 4-piston calipers.
dbCatalog.comp_note: CARRY-OVER (probable): hydraulic 180mm set continues on the Aventure 3; merged with the existing Aventure/Abound row. If teardowns later show a caliper change, split then. No used-set solds.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 346Wh battery (Soltera)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The 346Wh pack is the bike's weak spot. A spare in the pannier doubles a thin range.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Small 36V pack worth well under the 48V units. No confirmed solds; priced low against generic 36V packs at $120-180 new. 36V 9.6Ah spec constant across the run (some reviews round to 360Wh; 36V x 9.6Ah = 345.6Wh); no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 700x38c stock tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Commuters flat on glass and grates. Marathons end that conversation.
dbCatalog.comp_note: 700x38c confirmed by reviews. No solds found for the OEM tire. Generic 700c takeoff pairs move at $15-25. (The reflective-sidewall Kenda 700x38c belongs to the separate Soltera 2.5.)

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 720Wh battery (Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Cargo miles with kids aboard eat watt-hours. Second pack or shorter school runs, pick one.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Same 720Wh capacity as Aventure but a model-specific SKU, so kept separate from the Aventure pack. Priced to match; no confirmed solds. Constant across the 2023-2025 run (the 36V packs belong to the separate SR/LR); no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 50mm cargo suspension fork (Abound)
dbCatalog.comp_note: 50mm switchable-lockout fork confirmed by OutdoorGearLab/EBR. Cargo-specific 20-inch fork with almost no cross-compatibility. Thin market both directions.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 20x2.4 cargo tires (pair)
dbCatalog.comp_note: No resale market found for stock 20-inch cargo takeoff tires. Nobody is buying these used.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon hydraulic dropper post (Abound)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Droppers have a real used market but this is an OEM unit with unknown cross-fit. Priced conservatively.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon cargo saddle (Abound)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Hauling kids means stop-and-go riding all day. Wide and sprung wins.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Standard saddle economics: high supply, low demand.

dbCatalog.name: SRAM NX Eagle 12-speed drivetrain takeoff
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: NX shifts fine until it is muddy. GX is the cheapest real improvement mid-drive torque deserves.
dbCatalog.comp_note: eBay NX Eagle groupsets list $125-559 new depending on completeness; a used 4-piece takeoff realistically clears ~$140. High-volume market but no confirmed sold prices in results.

dbCatalog.name: SRAM DB8 4-piston brake set
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: DB8s stop fine. Codes stop fine all the way down the mountain, every lap.
dbCatalog.comp_note: DB8s retail ~$95 per wheel new. Used takeoff sets should clear $80. Already 4-piston, so takeoffs mostly come from lever-feel preference swaps. DB8 is original-Ramblas only -- the 2025 ADV downgraded to DB6 (separate model), which slightly boosts DB8 takeoff appeal. No year split within the original run.

dbCatalog.name: Maxxis Rekon 29x2.4 tires (pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Rekons roll fast and wash out in corners. Minions bite. Standard eMTB swap.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Used takeoff Rekons routinely move at $25-35 per tire in classifieds. Priced a pair at $50; no confirmed sold triplet. Note: original Ramblas rims are not tubeless-ready (Ebike Escape ADV review), so takeoff tires here ran tubes.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon 708Wh battery (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Turbo mode on trails drains it in 30 miles. Spare pack in the truck doubles the ride day.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Newest and priciest Aventon pack but zero used-market history yet. Priced with the 48V packs to stay conservative. 708Wh spec constant across the 2024-2025 run; no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon MTB saddle (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Saddles are personal but the Volt is the safest default in mountain biking.
dbCatalog.comp_note: OEM saddle, personal-fit item, minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon lock-on MTB grips (Ramblas)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Thin OEM rubber beats your palms on descents. Proper MTB grips are cheap insurance.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Parts-bin value.

dbCatalog.name: Aventon Ramblas stock platform pedals
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Real pins, rebuildable, cheap. No reason to ride the stockers.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Throw-in pedals on an otherwise name-brand build. First swap for most buyers.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 692Wh battery (Discover 1)

dbCatalog.comp_note: UL cert helps resale but no confirmed solds surfaced. Velotric sells spares new (price not confirmed in results); priced low against the generic-pack ceiling. 48V 14.4Ah spec constant across the 2022-2024 run; no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric color LCD display (Discover 1 / Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Velotric sells replacements direct, which caps the used price. Buyers are crash-damage owners. No solds found. The 3.5in backlit LCD belongs to the separate Discover 1 Plus (2024); base Discover 1 kept this unit throughout -- no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock suspension fork (Discover 1)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 26x2.5 gumwall tires (pair)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock comfort saddle

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock ergonomic lock-on grips

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock alloy platform pedals

dbCatalog.name: Velotric rear rack (Discover)

dbCatalog.name: Specialized 700x47c tubeless tire (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 3.5in color display (Discover 2 / T1 / Nomad 2)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric stock suspension fork (Discover 2)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Discover 2)

dbCatalog.name: CST BFT 26x4 fat tires (pair)

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 691Wh battery (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Fat tires eat charge. Spare pack or shorter rides.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Samsung/LG 21700 cells and UL cert support resale, but no confirmed solds surfaced. Priced low with the Discover 1 pack. Same pack spec on the Nomad 1 Plus, so buyer pool spans both; no year split within Nomad 1.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric 80mm hydraulic fat fork (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Same swap as every fat hub-drive: air spring and real damping over the stock boinger.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set 180mm (Nomad 1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: 73 pounds of bike plus rider at 28mph. Brake like you mean it.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric sport saddle (T1)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Fitness posture wants a performance saddle, not a couch. Volt is the safe pick.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Sport saddle, personal-fit item, minimal resale.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric slick commuter tires (T1, pair)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: A 36lb commuter with flat protection is the whole point of the T1.
dbCatalog.comp_note: OEM slicks with no aftermarket following. Exact stock size unverified in sources, so priced at floor.

dbCatalog.name: Velotric hydraulic disc brake set (T1)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Hydraulic from the factory across the T1 family; takeoffs are crash-repair driven only. No year split evidenced.

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Light Bee stock seat (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron 48T stock rear sprocket and chain (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron stock cast footpegs (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Ultra Bee stock rear shock (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Ultra Bee stock inverted fork (takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: More travel and real damping for riders who actually jump the bike.

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Ultra Bee 19in knobby tire pair (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron Light Bee stock inverted fork (DNM takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The standard fork swap. Stock DNM legs flex and blow seals when you jump the thing.

Retail replacements run $300 plus but used pulls move slow; pegged low. Catalog name kept from v1 since it already identifies the DNM part.

dbCatalog.name: Sur-Ron/Segway 19in CST knobby tire pair (takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The go-to street conversion tire. Stock knobbies squirm on pavement.

dbCatalog.name: RadWagon 5 deck + running boards

dbCatalog.name: Schwalbe Super Moto-X 27.5x2.4 Tire Pair (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Tektro HD-E350 Hydraulic Brake Set (e-bike takeoff)
dbCatalog.comp_note: HD-E350 verified (EBReport). Merges with the existing commodity HD-E350 row (Ride1Up 700/LMT'D pool): new sets $80-130, takeoff sets ~$55.

dbCatalog.name: Ride1Up Stock Color LCD Display
dbCatalog.comp_note: KD218-class displays sell new for $45-70; used units only move as repair parts to owners who smashed theirs. Estimating $25. Color LCD documented at both launch (EBR) and end of run - no evidence of a display revision, no year split.

dbCatalog.name: Turris Stock Comfort Saddle
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Same story as every commuter: stock saddle lasts until the first long weekend ride.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Unbranded saddle, no comps exist; $15 optimistic estimate against generic used-saddle asks.

dbCatalog.name: Kenda 27.5x2.4 Commuter Tire Pair (takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: The classic Turris tire bump: quieter, tougher, and it rolls faster than the stock Kendas.

dbCatalog.name: Shimano 7-Speed Shifter + Derailleur Set (Turris takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Twenty-five bucks kills the clunky thumb shifter. Cheapest quality-of-life fix on the bike.
dbCatalog.comp_note: Launch review confirms an Altus rear mech (not Tourney) paired with a bottom-rung SIS thumb shifter - v1's 'Tourney/Altus' hedge tightened to Altus. Forum consensus is still to replace the clunky thumb shifter first, which tells you what the takeoff is worth: near nothing.

dbCatalog.name: Ride1Up Prodigy 36V 14Ah Battery (used)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: Proprietary pack, so OEM is the only sane option. Second battery doubles commute range.
dbCatalog.comp_note: OEM replacement is $395 new from Ride1Up. Proprietary fit props up used value for Prodigy owners specifically, but the buyer pool is small. No solds found; estimating $175.

dbCatalog.name: Maxxis 27.5x2.4 Tire Pair (Prodigy takeoff)
dbCatalog.upgrade_note: For the all-weather commuter who bought a Brose bike to ride it every single day.

dbCatalog.name: Tektro HD-M745 4-Piston Brake Set (takeoff)
dbCatalog.comp_note: Real 4-piston brakes with genuine MTB demand; new sets run $170-200. No used solds pulled, so estimating $80 for a complete takeoff set. Exact HD-M745 model number is carried from Prodigy V1 reviews; V2 coverage confirms 4-piston Tektro 180mm but names the model less consistently.

dbCatalog.name: Prodigy Stock 100mm Air Fork (takeoff)

dbCatalog.name: Ride1Up 700 Series 48V Reention Rhino Battery (used)

dbCatalog.name: Stock Plastic Platform Pedals (takeoff)
